tracks.description: Builds and applies engineering craft, ranging from core CS fundamentals through deep system architecture and industry-shaping innovation

milestones.summary: Builds a foundation in core engineering concepts and ramps up on the team's primary stack (IC3 - Associate Software Engineer)
signals: Holds a working grasp of foundational computer science topics, including distributed systems vocabulary, functional programming patterns, and the CAP theorem, along with other concepts the team relies on day to day
Writes well-tested, idiomatic code and keeps side effects clearly separated from pure functional code

Picks up frequently used libraries and design patterns: producing and consuming message-queue events, leveraging existing design-system components, applying hexagonal architecture, and interacting with databases and HTTP APIs
examples: Walked through the team's onboarding doc and shipped a small first contribution end to end
Refactored a function during code review to separate I/O from pure logic
Added a new message-queue consumer following the squad's existing pattern

milestones.summary: Writes correct, clean code with light supervision and grows reach across more than one area (IC4 - Software Engineer)
signals: Produces correct, clean code with periodic guidance and reliably layers in unit, functional, and integration tests

Becomes self-sufficient in one substantial slice of the codebase or infrastructure (a cluster of microservices, a mobile product, or a meaningful frontend area) while keeping a working map of how neighbouring systems interact
Begins broadening into adjacent disciplines such as data, mobile, web, infrastructure, or distributed systems while continuing to deepen a primary specialization (T-shape or paint-drip model)
Contributes to shared libraries, platform tools, deployment pipelines, common rollout machinery, and design-system components
Navigates unfamiliar parts of the codebase with light guidance from the owners
Debugs methodically, driving toward root cause rather than relying on trial and error
examples: Took an unfamiliar service on call and resolved an incident by following the runbook
Opened a PR against a platform repository owned by another team
Reproduced a flaky bug deterministically before proposing a fix

milestones.summary: Owns whole features end to end, makes informed architectural trade-offs, and is the go-to expert in one area (IC5 - Senior Software Engineer)
signals: Demonstrates strong working knowledge of relevant infrastructure, service architectures, libraries, frameworks, and continuous-deployment systems
Reasons fluently about concurrency, multithreading, distributed systems, and microservice boundaries, and anticipates subtle failure modes before they reach production
Recognized as the expert in one area of the codebase or infrastructure while holding a clear mental model of the wider system
Performs and guides methodical, data-driven performance and scalability work
Notices and pushes back on unnecessary deviations from established patterns, improving consistency across the codebase

Justifies adding new technical surface area, such as platforms or infrastructure other engineers must learn, with concrete benefits rather than novelty, and aligns relevant stakeholders before introducing it
examples: Designed a caching strategy for a hot read path with documented latency and failure-mode trade-offs
Diagnosed a race condition spanning three services and authored the playbook for similar incidents
Vetoed a proposal to add a new database after running a benchmark showing the existing one would suffice

milestones.summary: Sought out for technical guidance across squads and demonstrates deep expertise in a domain or technology (IC6 - Lead Software Engineer)
signals: Treated as the technical reference point on their squad and on adjacent ones, anticipating product-level technical issues and shaping architecture and design choices to head them off
Holds deep domain or technical expertise on par with subject-matter specialists, with the operational, debugging, and tuning skills that go well beyond a typical user of the technology
Conceives and creates abstractions, libraries, and tools whose usefulness extends well past the original problem that prompted them
Debugs the gnarliest issues the team encounters, especially those crossing project, service, and timing boundaries
examples: Designed a shared client library now used by five squads
Led the postmortem on a multi-service race condition that had blocked the team for two weeks
Brought in a domain expert to review a contract and pushed back on requirements based on technical realities

milestones.summary: Provides technical direction across multiple teams and ships horizontal platforms or major infrastructure (E4 - Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Provides technical guidance across a broad swath of the system or as a recognized specialist in one of its core technologies
Picks up new areas and guides others fluently across large sections of the codebase
Shapes vertical domain architectures, delivers horizontal platforms, or ships complex libraries and major pieces of infrastructure
examples: Drove the design and rollout of a horizontal authorization platform consumed by every squad
Mentored leads from three different teams on architectural decisions that crossed system boundaries
Authored an RFC adopted across a business unit for handling cross-service idempotency

milestones.summary: Anticipates technical change at the company level and ties technical capabilities to business strategy (E5 - Senior Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Anticipates broad technical change and scaling challenges, keeping the engineering organization ahead of the curve
Recognizes and reasons through trade-offs at the level of the whole system
Holds a deep, end-to-end mental model of the organization's architecture and articulates scaling and reliability limits holistically
Connects technical capabilities to product and service competitiveness in the market, combining technical and business intuition
examples: Authored a multi-year scaling roadmap covering reliability, throughput, and cost
Identified a class of architectural risk three quarters ahead of customer impact and led the mitigation work
Translated an ambiguous executive concern into a concrete platform investment thesis

milestones.summary: Creates abstractions, patterns, and tools with applicability beyond the company, and contributes credibly anywhere (E6 - Principal Software Engineer)
signals: Creates and identifies abstractions, patterns, frameworks, and tools whose value extends to other developers and other companies in the same industry
Holds enough breadth to come up to speed and contribute meaningfully in essentially any technical area of the organization
examples: Authored a framework or specification adopted outside the organization
Joined a new domain and was producing high-leverage work within a single quarter
Identified a class of problem recurring across multiple business units and shipped a single abstraction that retired all of them

milestones.summary: Recognized industry-wide as a leading expert in or the creator of an area of specialty (E7 - Distinguished Software Engineer)
signals: Easily recognizable as one of the world's leading experts in, or the creator of, their area of specialty
examples: Originated a widely adopted technology, paper, or industry technique
Recognized externally with awards, keynotes, or fellowship-level honors in the field
Sets technical direction that other companies and engineers explicitly follow

tracks.description: Translates ambiguous problems into shipped, observable outcomes through disciplined planning, execution, and operational ownership

milestones.summary: Builds productivity skills and ships well-defined tasks all the way to production (IC3 - Associate Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Low-ambiguity tasks with Low autonomy — manager or more tenured engineers lead 50%+ of feature decisions
Develops productivity skills by learning the team's tooling, including source control, editors, the continuous deployment pipeline, logging, monitoring, and testing best practices
Takes well-defined tasks and delivers them all the way through to production, collaborating with teammates where needed
Holds practical, hands-on knowledge of how the team ships, including how services deploy, how the app is released, how to change a rollout, and how to read monitoring dashboards
Monitors services owned by the team across logs, dashboards, and other sources and reacts appropriately by taking on-call shifts, escalating, calling a crash, and debugging through to resolution
examples: Joined the on-call rotation and successfully handled a paging incident during the shift
Pushed a small change through CI/CD without manual hand-holding
Read a dashboard during an incident and identified the spiking metric

milestones.summary: Makes steady progress on tasks, communicates assumptions early, and unblocks themselves and the team (IC4 - Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Low-ambiguity work executed independently and Medium-ambiguity work with guidance — manager leads 30-50% of feature decisions
Makes steady, predictable progress and recognizes when to ask for help and when to offer it to keep the team unblocked
Understands the team's domain model and the business context surrounding it
Sidesteps bikeshedding and focuses energy on the parts of a problem with the most impact or risk
Surfaces assumptions and pushes for clarification on tasks before starting, reducing rework
Picks up issues proactively rather than waiting for work to be assigned
examples: Identified a missing requirement during sprint planning that would have caused rework
Volunteered to investigate a long-standing TODO when their primary work was blocked
Wrote a one-paragraph design note that surfaced an assumption the PM ultimately disagreed with

milestones.summary: De-risks design choices empirically, ships in well-staged milestones, and keeps the team's systems healthy (IC5 - Senior Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Low-to-Medium ambiguity executed with High autonomy — manager leads under 30% of feature decisions
Backs significant design or architectural choices with empirical evidence such as proofs of concept, tests, and external research, given a clear product motivation
Drives small-to-medium features from technical design through one hundred percent rollout, including bug fixing and post-launch improvements
Breaks work into well-defined milestones rather than producing a single monolithic deliverable
Manages expectations with stakeholders along the way
Removes technical roadblocks for the team, such as fixing flaky pipelines, pruning noisy alerts, driving down exception volume, and adding analytical controls and monitoring
Owns system stability by ensuring testing and observability are in place, watching production behaviour, and reacting quickly to incidents
examples: Ran a two-week proof of concept that decided a contested architectural choice
Cut the team's pager volume in half over a quarter
Staged a feature behind feature flags and rolled it out in three controlled steps

milestones.summary: Reduces complexity, manages technical debt deliberately, and rolls out sensitive changes safely (IC6 - Lead Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Medium ambiguity driven from definition through rollout with High autonomy — manager provides context rather than direction
Consistently reduces the complexity of projects, systems, and processes to get more done with less work, breaking up deliverables for early feedback and incremental business value
Spots accumulating technical debt and proactively influences the team to pay it down before it grows
Plans and rolls out changes through to completion with controlled, low impact on customers and downstream systems, partnering with PM, BA, and other roles as needed
examples: Killed a planned multi-quarter project by proposing a much smaller alternative that hit the same outcome
Authored and led a tech-debt initiative that landed in a single sprint and removed a chronic source of incidents
Coordinated a sensitive data migration with zero customer-visible impact

milestones.summary: Plans and executes highly sensitive cross-system changes and creates leadership opportunities for others (E4 - Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: High ambiguity with Very High autonomy — defines problem, plan, and trade-offs with effectively no day-to-day managerial direction
Plans and rolls out highly sensitive system changes through to completion with controlled, low impact on customers and client systems, partnering with PM, BA, and other roles
Proactively identifies and starts projects to reshape architecture when preconditions have changed and the existing design no longer fits, extracting and documenting platforms as needed
Spots opportunities for engineers to take leadership on increasingly larger or more sensitive projects, partnering with managers to set them up for success
examples: Led a year-long migration spanning four squads with no major outage and a clear weekly burn-down
Identified that a mature platform should be extracted into its own service and led that extraction
Recommended an engineer for a tech-lead role on a high-stakes project and supported them through it

milestones.summary: Determines the most important technical risks and opportunities and drives waves of change to completion (E5 - Senior Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: High-to-Very-High ambiguity with Very High autonomy — frames problems and trade-offs spanning multiple business units
Determines the organization's most important technical risks and opportunities and executes against them efficiently
Drives waves of technical change all the way through rollout, owning execution and not just vision
Builds architecture that keeps many futures open without requiring certainty about which one will arrive
Develops a pipeline of future staff engineers by spotting technical talent and providing focused mentorship
examples: Owned a company-wide technical initiative from kickoff through complete rollout across every squad
Architected a system whose core abstractions survived three product pivots
Mentored two engineers through promotion to staff over an eighteen-month period

milestones.summary: Produces a sustained stream of valuable software and balances community priorities with company outcomes (E6 - Principal Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Very High ambiguity — frames problems no one else has framed, requiring deep customer, market, or technical-strategy context
Prolific producer of valuable open- and closed-source software
Balances broader community priorities against the company's success
examples: Maintains a project relied on across the organization and adopted externally
Negotiated a contribution to an external open-source project that solved an internal need
Made a principled call to prioritize an internal need over an external community ask and explained the reasoning publicly

milestones.summary: Holds broad visibility outside the company through invention, implementation, writing, and speaking (E7 - Distinguished Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: industry-defining ambiguity — problems often have no precedent or playbook
Holds broad visibility outside the company via invention, implementation, writing, or speaking
examples: Delivered a keynote at a major industry conference
Authored a paper or article cited across the field
Built a tool used by engineering teams across the industry

tracks.description: Drives business and technical outcomes whose value compounds across teams, products, and the broader engineering organization

milestones.summary: Focuses on growing as an engineer and on living the team's core values (IC3 - Associate Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Low complexity — one team, one business line, services within the squad
Focuses energy on growing as an engineer, quickly absorbing existing tools, resources, and processes
Embodies the team's core values, putting effort into understanding and living them
examples: Came in with thoughtful questions during onboarding
Asked for and applied feedback after each shipped task
Volunteered for a values-related team initiative

milestones.summary: Contributes to technical design and connects individual work to wider business impact (IC4 - Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Low-to-Medium complexity — single team or 2-3 teams within one business line; services starting to be consumed by adjacent teams
Actively contributes to the technical design of features
Owns features end to end with growing complexity, from inception through production into post-launch monitoring
Reasons through and articulates well-considered design trade-offs in their area
Sees how individual contributions roll up into larger business impact and flags issues with requirements or customer requests early
Looks for ways to improve the design and code quality of the team's systems
examples: Caught a contradictory requirement in a spec before development started
Followed a feature into production and tracked the metrics it was meant to move
Refactored a long-standing piece of legacy code as part of regular feature work

milestones.summary: Partners cross-functionally, drives requirements that include all parties, and improves core platforms or critical logic (IC5 - Senior Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Medium complexity — 2-3 teams within one business line (or one team across multiple lines); owns services consumed by 2+ adjacent teams
Partners with analysts, data scientists, designers, product peers, and other specialists to shape requirements that account for everyone's needs
Makes material improvements to core platform code or to critical business logic for the organization
Lets customer empathy guide decisions and avoids building things that do not solve real problems
Spots risks and problems in their own work and in others' work
Takes initiative to identify and solve important problems, coordinating with others on cross-cutting technical issues
Helps shape team objectives such as OKRs from an engineering perspective when relevant
examples: Reframed a feature spec after a working session with the analyst that exposed a missing data assumption
Proposed and led a refactor of a critical pricing component that paid off across squads
Owned an OKR jointly with the PM and brought engineering rigor to the metric definition

milestones.summary: Recognized as a prolific contributor; articulates trade-offs and creates sweeping system improvements (IC6 - Lead Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: High complexity — multiple business lines and 2-3 teams in flight; owns services used across business-line boundaries
Recognized by business-unit technical leadership as a prolific contributor to core and side projects
Articulates the trade-offs between technical, analytical, and product needs, including efficiency versus thoroughness and taking on debt versus paying it down
Has a track record of sweeping improvements in stability, performance, clarity, and scalability across major systems
Has visibly improved several projects or teams
examples: Led a quarter-long initiative that reduced overall service latency by an order of magnitude
Wrote and presented a trade-off analysis that decided a contested cross-team direction
Joined a struggling project as a contributor and turned its trajectory around

milestones.summary: Operates as an engineering stakeholder at the BU level, multiplies team effectiveness, and shifts the organization's technical trajectory (E4 - Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: High-to-Very-High complexity — 3+ teams coordinating across multiple business lines; owns services consumed by many teams and 2+ business units

Acts as an engineering stakeholder at the business-unit level and consistently influences decision-making across multiple teams, with awareness of how those teams interact with other parts of the business
Multiplies others' effectiveness by aligning teams on a platform vision and creating building blocks that unlock faster, better future deliveries
Has visibly shifted the organization's overall technical trajectory
examples: Sat in business-unit leadership planning and represented engineering interests through to the final plan
Authored a platform vision document that aligned three squads on a year of work
Built or championed a foundational system whose absence would now be unimaginable

milestones.summary: Drives multi-year strategy across critical business areas and serves as a role model and force multiplier for the org (E5 - Senior Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: Very High complexity — initiatives span the engineering organization and 2+ business units
Plays a key role in shaping a multi-year technology strategy for entire critical areas of the business spanning multiple systems and teams
Decisions made directly affect the long-term success or failure of the organization
Seen as a role model and mentor by many technical members of the team
Acts primarily as a multiplier through systems built, tools authored, or policies and patterns introduced that lift the productivity of the entire org
examples: Co-authored a three-year technology strategy adopted by senior leadership
Mentored a cohort of senior engineers, several of whom went on to staff roles
Introduced a process or pattern, such as a design-doc culture or RFC review, that the entire org now uses

milestones.summary: Directly and quantifiably moves the business and contributes substantially to influential open-source projects (E6 - Principal Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: company-wide breadth — outcomes touch the whole engineering organization and reach the external community
Directly and quantifiably impacts the business as a whole
Consistently produces significant contributions to influential open-source projects that shape the broader software industry
examples: Delivered an initiative whose business impact appeared on a leadership scorecard
Maintains or core-contributes to a widely used open-source project
Created a piece of internal infrastructure later released as open source and adopted broadly

milestones.summary: Influences technical direction beyond the company and shapes its global reputation (E7 - Distinguished Software Engineer)
signals: Typical scope: industry-wide — outcomes shape problems beyond the company
Influences technical direction beyond the organization itself
Plays a critical role in establishing the organization's reputation as one of the well-known global technology companies
examples: Sets technical direction that other companies follow
Repeatedly invited to shape industry standards or working groups
Drives initiatives that materially elevate the organization's external technical brand

tracks.description: Influences peers, partners, and leaders through clear writing, productive collaboration, mentorship, and a trusted technical voice

milestones.summary: Collaborates effectively, asks for help well, and practices egoless programming (IC3 - Associate Software Engineer)
signals: Collaborates effectively with the team by pairing, asking for help, communicating status and blockers, and contributing to the continuous improvement of team processes
Practices egoless programming, accepts feedback graciously, and looks for opportunities to pair and mob with others
examples: Asked for help quickly when stuck rather than spinning silently
Took critical code-review feedback gracefully and reflected it in the next PR
Joined a mob session and contributed instead of just observing

milestones.summary: Drives small-to-medium features, gives helpful feedback, and mentors newer teammates (IC4 - Software Engineer)
signals: Collaborates to drive small-to-medium features from technical design through completion
Gives timely, helpful feedback to peers and managers
Makes progress without needing repeated significant rework or oversight
Persistent in the face of roadblocks, dispatching them efficiently and pulling others in when needed, and works with minimal supervision
Proactively asks others for feedback and is eager to find ways to improve
Communicates technical decisions and outcomes visibly through design docs, tech talks, demos, or similar artifacts
Mentors more junior or newer engineers via pairing, mobbing, design review, and code review, and reviews the team's relevant pull requests
Helps with at least one of recruiting, interviewing, onboarding, documentation, open source, remote work, conferences, or blog posts
examples: Wrote a clear design doc that became the spec for a feature the whole team built
Onboarded a new hire and walked them through their first PRs
Volunteered to interview candidates and gave thoughtful written feedback

milestones.summary: Communicates effectively across functions, pushes back constructively, and multiplies others through teaching (IC5 - Senior Software Engineer)
signals: Communicates effectively across functions inside the team, interacts productively with adjacent disciplines, and partners with leaders across their scope

Proactively flags problems with requirements, such as lack of clarity, inconsistencies, or technical limits, and helps course-correct early; understands what is meant beyond what is literally said when interacting with other functions; participates in product discussions
Disagrees constructively and pushes back when needed, including with more senior or tenured colleagues
Proactively seeks constructive criticism on design and architecture from teammates and other engineers
Masters whiteboarding and documentation techniques to convey ideas efficiently to other engineers, and judges when those artifacts should be durable and shared
Multiplies team impact by making others better through code reviews, whiteboarding, technical guidance, mentoring, tech talks, and training, and is enjoyable to collaborate with in those settings
Participates in the continuous improvement of discipline-wide processes such as recruiting, hiring, onboarding, documentation, open source, and remote work
examples: Pushed back on a feature scope decision in a product review and proposed a smaller alternative the team adopted
Ran a brown-bag tech talk that the discipline still references months later
Refactored the discipline's onboarding document based on what new hires were stumbling on

milestones.summary: Engineering stakeholder at team level, frequently consulted across squads, and guides architectural conversations (IC6 - Lead Software Engineer)
signals: Acts as an engineering stakeholder at the team level and consistently influences decision-making across adjacent teams, participating actively in product discussions
Frequently asked into architecture reviews and provides feedback on projects outside their core area
Guides the architecture of a subdomain of the system and facilitates discussions that help their team and neighbours ship complex projects

Contributes both to non-engineer-facing technical documentation, so partners can understand what the system does at a high level, and to lower-level engineer-facing documentation that flows out of whiteboarding and technical discussions
Multiplies the effectiveness of others by facilitating cross-team work or accelerating knowledge transfer through documentation, tutorials, or talks
Listens to and guides technical debates toward consensus, regularly framing the conversation one level of abstraction above the immediate problem
examples: Chaired the architecture review for an inter-team project and produced a clear written outcome
Wrote the canonical 'how this subdomain works' document referenced by every onboarding engineer
Helped two squads converge on a shared API after a stalled debate

milestones.summary: Engineering stakeholder at BU level, sought out for big project assessments, and sets 6 to 12 month technical direction (E4 - Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Acts as an engineering stakeholder at the business-unit level and consistently influences decision-making across multiple teams, with a clear understanding of how the BU interacts with other parts of the organization
Frequently asked to assess the technical challenges of large projects and to participate in product and business decisions alongside leaders from other disciplines
Leads and participates in tech forums, identifying problems that affect multiple teams and creating alignment across the engineering org
Sets short- and medium-term strategic technical direction, looking six to twelve months ahead to identify the areas of greatest need and producing concrete plans to address them
examples: Co-led a business-unit technology forum that produced a quarterly engineering roadmap
Wrote a twelve-month plan for paying down a class of architectural risk and drove it through to delivery
Was the engineering signatory on a cross-functional initiative that crossed BU boundaries

milestones.summary: Builds the engineering brand externally, leads internal direction-setting, and earns executive trust (E5 - Senior Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Builds the engineering brand externally through talks and blog posts, identifying which areas can be shared effectively with the outside world and shaping the resulting content and communication
Leads internal conversations about the direction of major technology areas, drives wide consensus on adoption, and uses that direction to inspire engineers
Holds productive conversations with executives, cutting through jargon and information asymmetry to inspire trust and confidence
examples: Delivered a conference talk that became the headline external example of the engineering org's work
Drove company-wide alignment on a major technology direction through a series of open conversations
Briefed the executive team on a complex technical topic and earned approval for a sizeable investment

milestones.summary: Communicates with skill across every audience and adjusts style fluidly to context (E6 - Principal Software Engineer)
signals: Excellent communicator across all audiences, adjusting style and depth to the audience and the situation
examples: Delivered the same complex idea credibly to engineers, executives, and customers in a single week
Authored a public-facing piece referenced inside and outside the organization
Mediated a high-stakes alignment meeting between leaders with conflicting priorities

milestones.summary: Helps engineers at every level become better and is widely valued for advice and mentorship (E7 - Distinguished Software Engineer)
signals: Helps engineers at every level become better and is widely valued for advice and mentorship
examples: Sought out by engineers across many teams for career and technical advice
Mentored multiple staff and principal engineers through their careers
Asked to participate in critical hiring and leveling decisions throughout the organization

tracks.description: Adopts, integrates, and shapes the use of AI tooling and AI-powered systems across the engineering practice, from day-to-day assistance through industry-defining capabilities

milestones.summary: Uses AI tools as part of day-to-day workflow with light supervision (IC3 - Associate Software Engineer)
signals: Uses code assistants such as Copilot, Cursor, or Claude Code in the IDE for routine tasks like scaffolding, boilerplate, and explanations
Asks AI for help understanding unfamiliar code, error messages, or library APIs and verifies the answer before acting on it
Follows the team's guidelines around what data, code, and credentials may be shared with AI assistants
examples: Used an AI assistant to scaffold unit tests for a new function and reviewed every assertion before merging
Asked Claude to explain a regex from the codebase and confirmed the explanation against the actual matches
Followed the team's policy when configuring an AI extension, opting out of model training on company code

milestones.summary: Applies AI thoughtfully across the SDLC and recognizes when output cannot be trusted (IC4 - Software Engineer)
signals: Writes prompts that include the right context, constraints, and acceptance criteria rather than one-line questions
Validates AI-generated code with tests, type checks, and a careful read instead of merging on faith
Recognizes hallucinations, fabricated APIs, and out-of-date suggestions and corrects course quickly
Reaches for AI to debug, refactor, and explore alternatives, while still understanding the change before shipping
examples: Refactored a legacy module with AI assistance, expanding test coverage as part of the change
Caught a hallucinated function call in a PR suggested by an AI assistant and rewrote the diff
Used an AI agent to enumerate edge cases for a feature spec and turned them into test fixtures

milestones.summary: Designs AI-assisted workflows for the team and reasons about model trade-offs (IC5 - Senior Software Engineer)
signals: Builds reusable prompts, slash commands, or repo-level agent configs that lift the whole team's leverage
Integrates AI into team workflows where it pays off (PR triage, doc generation, incident summaries) and skips it where it does not
Articulates trade-offs across models in terms of cost, latency, quality, and privacy, and picks the right tool for the job
Spots when AI is the wrong answer and pushes back instead of bolting it onto every problem
examples: Authored a CLAUDE.md or repo prompt template that became the team's default for new contributors
Ran a controlled bake-off between two models for a feature and documented the decision
Vetoed an AI-powered feature proposal after showing a deterministic rule covered the use case

milestones.summary: Owns AI capability for a domain end to end, including evals and cost-quality trade-offs (IC6 - Lead Software Engineer)
signals: Designs and operates evaluation harnesses that catch regressions in prompts, models, and agent behaviour before they ship
Builds production-grade AI features (RAG, tool-using agents, structured-output pipelines) with observability, retries, and graceful degradation
Manages cost, latency, and quality at scale through caching, prompt compression, model routing, or fine-tuning where it pays off
Recognized as the go-to reviewer for AI-related designs and PRs across the squad
examples: Led the design of an agentic workflow that replaced a manual ops process and reduced cycle time meaningfully
Built an eval suite that blocked a model upgrade after catching a quality regression on a critical task
Cut LLM spend on a hot path by an order of magnitude through prompt caching and model routing without quality loss

milestones.summary: Sets AI engineering patterns across multiple teams and owns model and platform strategy (E4 - Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Defines org-wide patterns for AI-assisted development and AI-powered features, including secure data handling, evaluation discipline, and rollout playbooks
Builds shared platform pieces — eval infra, prompt registries, agent runtimes, gateway and observability — used by every squad shipping AI
Owns the model upgrade and provider strategy, including build-vs-buy calls and migration plans across model families
Anticipates platform implications of new AI capabilities (latency, cost curves, data residency, prompt injection) before they bite production
examples: Authored an RFC adopted across the business unit on safe agent design, including tool sandboxing and human-in-the-loop checkpoints
Drove a coordinated migration from one model family to another with a regression-tested rollout across five squads
Stood up a shared LLM gateway with logging, redaction, and cost attribution that became the only sanctioned path to providers

milestones.summary: Connects AI capability to multi-year business strategy and emergent risk (E5 - Senior Staff Software Engineer)
signals: Plays a key role in shaping a multi-year AI strategy spanning product, platform, data, and security
Anticipates broad shifts — context windows, agent autonomy, pricing models, regulation — and keeps the org ahead of the curve
Identifies emergent risks early (data leakage, prompt injection, model collapse, attribution disputes) and leads mitigation work
Influences hiring, training, and ways-of-working so the entire engineering org levels up its AI fluency
examples: Co-authored a three-year AI strategy adopted by senior leadership, with concrete platform and product bets
Identified a class of prompt-injection risk three quarters ahead of customer impact and led the cross-team mitigation
Designed and rolled out an AI-fluency curriculum that measurably shifted productivity across the engineering org

milestones.summary: Creates AI engineering patterns whose value extends beyond the company (E6 - Principal Software Engineer)
signals: Creates abstractions, frameworks, or evaluations whose usefulness extends to other developers and other companies in the industry
Holds enough breadth to come up to speed and contribute meaningfully across any AI surface — assistants, agents, RAG, fine-tuning, evals, safety
Shapes industry conversations on AI engineering practice through writing, talks, or reference implementations
examples: Open-sourced an evaluation framework or agent runtime adopted by teams outside the company
Authored a reference architecture for production AI systems referenced by peers in the industry
Joined a new AI sub-discipline (e.g. agent reliability, evals, retrieval) and was producing high-leverage work within a quarter

milestones.summary: Recognized industry-wide as a leading voice in AI engineering (E7 - Distinguished Software Engineer)
signals: Easily recognizable as one of the world's leading practitioners in AI engineering or in a specific AI sub-discipline
Sets technical direction for AI engineering practice that other companies and engineers explicitly follow
examples: Originated a widely adopted AI engineering technique, paper, or open-source project
Recognized externally with awards, keynotes, or fellowship-level honors specifically for work on AI systems
Sets technical direction on AI that other companies and engineers explicitly follow
